rules.description: Analyze and strictly adhere to the existing architecture, linting conventions, and project standards. Do not introduce new patterns without explicit guidance.
Perform a complete semantic search across the codebase before generating new code. Always prefer reusing existing components, functions, and services.
Apply the DRY principle: whenever you detect repeated logic, extract it into a reusable function, method, or component.
Structure every new module following SOLID and the Clean Architecture layers identified in the project (domain, application, infrastructure, interfaces).
When a task begins with a failing test (TDD workflow), follow the sequence: 1) confirm the test fails, 2) implement the simplest code to make it pass, 3) propose refactors if applicable.
For BDD workflows, whenever a specification (e.g., Gherkin) is provided, generate the step definitions and application logic necessary to satisfy the acceptance criteria.
Maintain technology consistency. Only use languages, frameworks, and libraries that already exist in the project manifests (e.g., package.json, pom.xml, requirements.txt).
Create or update documentation (docstrings, JSDoc, KDoc, etc.) for every public function or class you modify, explaining purpose, parameters, and return values.
At the end of every change, provide a technical changelog summarizing what was done, which files were touched, and the reasoning.
When editing existing code, analyze the surrounding context. If you notice code smells or optimization opportunities, propose refactors and explain the benefits.
Whenever a commit message is requested, follow the Conventional Commits format (feat, fix, refactor, etc.) based on the actual change.
All communication and explanations in the assistant conversation must be in Brazilian Portuguese.
All documentation and comments in the code must be written in English.
Always explain what was changed and the rationale behind each edit.
Preserve the existing structure. Do not rearrange files or modules unless it is absolutely necessary.
Search the entire codebase before altering or implementing new logic.
Do not create new files if related ones already exist. Reuse or extend the current artifacts whenever possible.
Keep the overall structure organized at all times.
Before adding a new dependency, verify if an equivalent is already available in the project.
Respect the project's established languages and technologies (e.g., Tailwind CSS).
